message.content:
CONFIGURED-panel review (one of 2; return verdict JSON, write NOTHING). Round 1.
Task: T78 (goals:G5) — "Realhosts report-only dial-by-name check (stretch)". Report-only per the M10/Q12 discipline.
acceptance: go test -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/... runs against the standing testbed and emits the dial-by-name report (resolution result + handshake + traffic outcome); an induced failure (bogus name) demonstrably does NOT fail the suite — it reports and passes.
Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a6ff3c7de32c86767 (branch implement/T78, base 356d850, HEAD 12a238a). Gate via `nix develop -c sh -c '...'`. The -tags realhosts suite runs against the STANDING testbed (not runnable here) — judge by compile/vet under -tags realhosts + inspection against the existing test/realhosts harness.
Worker: pass; new file test/realhosts/dns_dial_test.go (TestRealDNSDialByName). Report-only: resolution/handshake/traffic outcomes use t.Logf only (never t.Fatalf/Errorf); TUN/daemon bring-up stays fatal (matches sibling tiers). Bogus name = RFC-2606 .example TLD. Parses the "first endpoint resolution" journal line.
JUDGE: (a) is the REPORT-ONLY discipline correct — do the dial-by-name OUTCOME assertions (resolution/handshake/traffic) genuinely NEVER fail the suite (t.Logf only), while a bogus-name subtest still reports+passes? Grep the test for any t.Fatalf/t.Errorf/t.Fail on the outcome path. (b) does it MATCH the existing test/realhosts discipline (read runner.go / a sibling like smoke_test.go / soak_test.go — same report-line style, same fatal-only-on-infra-bringup)? (c) is the "first endpoint resolution" journal-marker parse correct against production source (failover.go)? (d) does the bogus-name .example choice actually pass config.validateHostname (so the daemon starts) yet deterministically NXDOMAIN? (e) compiles/vets under -tags realhosts + full non-realhosts gate green. Return { taskId, verdict, criticism: [], questions: [], defects: [], rationale, summary }.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Let me read the new test, a sibling test, the runner, and the production failover source.

content.content:
1	//go:build realhosts
2	
3	package realhosts
4	
5	import (
6		"context"
7		"encoding/json"
8		"fmt"
9		"strings"
10		"testing"
11		"time"
12	)
13	
14	// DNS dial-by-name overlay addressing/wire parameters (Q36, stretch). Distinct inner
15	// addresses, port, and systemd units from the smoke/soak/failover tiers so a run of
16	// several tiers back to back never collides on a leftover interface or unit.
17	const (
18		dnsEdgeInner  = "10.10.1.1"
19		dnsConcInner  = "10.10.1.2"
20		dnsListenPort = 51840
21	
22		dnsConcUnit = "wanbond-dns-conc"
23		dnsEdgeUnit = "wanbond-dns-edge"
24	
25		// dnsBogusHost is a syntactically valid (passes config.validateHostname) but
26		// deliberately unresolvable hostname under the IANA-reserved "example" TLD
27		// (RFC 2606 guarantees it is never delegated), so the induced failure this
28		// subtest demonstrates is deterministic and touches no real DNS
29		// infrastructure.
30		dnsBogusHost = "this-host-does-not-exist.invalid.wanbond-dns-test.example"
31	)
32	
33	// Timeouts specific to this tier. dnsHandshakeTimeout is generous (a real resolver
34	// round trip plus the real NAT path) but the tier waits at most this long before
35	// REPORTING — never failing — a stalled resolution/handshake.
36	const (
37		dnsHandshakeTimeout = 45 * time.Second
38		dnsTrafficTimeout   = 20 * time.Second
39	)
40	
41	// TestRealDNSDialByName is the report-only dial-by-name stretch tier (Q36, M10/Q12
42	// discipline): it brings up the standing two-host testbed with the EDGE dialing the
43	// concentrator by its real DNS name (cfg.Conc.Addr — defaultConcHost, o3.7mind.io,
44	// which resolves to the standing ConcPubIP) with the peer's `dns = true` opt-in
45	// enabled, then repeats the same bring-up against a deliberately bogus hostname.
46	// Both scenarios REPORT their outcome (resolved address, time-to-first-handshake,
47	// and a steady-traffic sample) and NEVER fail the suite on that outcome — only the
48	// SSH/provisioning/build plumbing (a precondition, not the thing under test) can
49	// fail the test, mirroring every other tier in this package. There is no mid-session
50	// IP change: the concentrator's public IP is the fixed, standing ConcPubIP; this
51	// tier only proves the resolve-then-dial path against a real resolver and real NAT.
52	func TestRealDNSDialByName(t *testing.T) {
53		cfg := LoadConfig()
54		r := NewRunner(cfg)
55	
56		t.Logf("realhosts config: edge=%s concentrator=%s conc-public-ip=%s ssh-key=%s",
57			cfg.Edge.target(), cfg.Conc.target(), cfg.ConcPubIP, cfg.SSHKey)
58	
59		provision(t, r, cfg.Edge, ProvisionOpts{})
60		provision(t, r, cfg.Conc, ProvisionOpts{TunnelIface: tunnelIface})
61	
62		root := repoRoot(t)
63		syncAndBuild(t, r, cfg.Edge, root)
64		t.Cleanup(func() { removeRemoteDir(t, r, cfg.Edge) })
65		syncAndBuild(t, r, cfg.Conc, root)
66		t.Cleanup(func() { removeRemoteDir(t, r, cfg.Conc) })
67	
68		edgePriv, edgePub := genSmokeKey(t)
69		concPriv, concPub := genSmokeKey(t)
70		psk := randSmokeKey(t)
71	
72		edgeSrc := primaryIP(t, r, cfg.Edge)
73		concSrc := primaryIP(t, r, cfg.Conc)
74		t.Logf("path source addrs: edge=%s concentrator=%s", edgeSrc, concSrc)
75	
76		// The concentrator listens on the real public endpoint (ConcPubIP:dnsListenPort)
77		// throughout BOTH subtests; only the edge's configured peer HOSTNAME changes
78		// between them, so one concentrator bring-up and one iperf3 server suffice.
79		startDNSConcentrator(t, r, cfg.Conc, concSrc, concPriv, edgePub, psk)
80		startIperfServer(t, r, cfg.Conc, dnsConcInner)
81	
82		t.Run("valid_name", func(t *testing.T) {
83			runDNSDialScenario(t, r, cfg.Edge, cfg.Conc.Addr, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, psk)
84		})
85		t.Run("bogus_name", func(t *testing.T) {
86			runDNSDialScenario(t, r, cfg.Edge, dnsBogusHost, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, psk)
87		})
88	}
89	
90	// startDNSConcentrator writes and starts the concentrator's dial-by-name-tier
91	// daemon (own config/unit/port, distinct from the smoke tier's), then waits for its
92	// TUN and addresses it. This is INFRASTRUCTURE the scenario is built on, not the
93	// dial-by-name outcome under test, so — matching the rest of this package — it
94	// Fatals on failure.
95	func startDNSConcentrator(t *testing.T, r *Runner, conc Host, concSrc, concPriv, edgePub, psk string) {
96		t.Helper()
97	
98		concCfg := fmt.Sprintf(`role = "concentrator"
99	psk = "%s"
100	
101	[[paths]]
102	name = "wan0"
103	source_addr = "%s"
104	
105	[wireguard]
106	private_key = "%s"
107	listen_port = %d
108	
109	[[wireguard.peers]]
110	public_key = "%s"
111	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
112	
113	[log]
114	level = "info"
115	`, psk, concSrc, concPriv, dnsListenPort, edgePub, dnsEdgeInner)
116	
117		concCfgPath := smokeRemoteDir + "/dns-conc.toml"
118		writeRemoteFile(t, r, conc, concCfgPath, concCfg)
119	
120		stopUnit(t, r, conc, dnsConcUnit)
121		delLink(t, r, conc, tunnelIface)
122		t.Cleanup(func() {
123			stopUnit(t, r, conc, dnsConcUnit)
124			delLink(t, r, conc, tunnelIface)
125		})
126	
127		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), smokeSSHTimeout)
128		defer cancel()
129		start := fmt.Sprintf("sudo systemd-run --unit=%s --service-type=simple %s --config %s",
130			dnsConcUnit, smokeBin, concCfgPath)
131		if _, err := r.Run(ctx, conc, start); err != nil {
132			t.Fatalf("concentrator: start wanbond daemon failed: %v", err)
133		}
134		t.Logf("concentrator: wanbond daemon started (unit=%s)", dnsConcUnit)
135	
136		if !waitRemoteLink(t, r, conc, tunnelIface, linkAppearTimeout) {
137			dumpUnitLog(t, r, conc, dnsConcUnit)
138			t.Fatalf("concentrator: %s never appeared", tunnelIface)
139		}
140		addressLink(t, r, conc, dnsConcInner)
141	}
142	
143	// runDNSDialScenario writes and starts the EDGE daemon dialing dialHost:dnsListenPort
144	// (the peer's `dns = true` opt-in enabled) against the already-running concentrator,
145	// then observes — but does NOT gate on — the resolve-then-dial outcome: the resolved
146	// address the daemon's own re-resolution controller installed (read from ITS journal,
147	// not a client-side lookup, so this proves wanbond's own resolver path rather than the
148	// test host's), the time-to-first-handshake, and a short best-effort traffic sample
149	// taken only once a handshake completed.
150	//
151	// Standing up the daemon and its TUN is a PRECONDITION (Fatal on failure, matching the
152	// rest of this package); the RESOLUTION / HANDSHAKE / TRAFFIC outcome itself never
153	// calls t.Fatalf/t.Errorf — only t.Logf. This is the acceptance property the
154	// "bogus_name" subtest exists to demonstrate: an unresolvable hostname reports a
155	// failed resolution/handshake/traffic outcome and the subtest still PASSES.
156	func runDNSDialScenario(t *testing.T, r *Runner, edge Host, dialHost, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, psk string) {
157		t.Helper()
158	
159		edgeCfg := fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
160	psk = "%s"
161	
162	[[paths]]
163	name = "wan0"
164	source_addr = "%s"
165	
166	[wireguard]
167	private_key = "%s"
168	
169	[[wireguard.peers]]
170	public_key = "%s"
171	endpoint = "%s:%d"
172	dns = true
173	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
174	
175	[log]
176	level = "info"
177	`, psk, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, dialHost, dnsListenPort, dnsConcInner)
178	
179		edgeCfgPath := smokeRemoteDir + "/dns-edge.toml"
180		writeRemoteFile(t, r, edge, edgeCfgPath, edgeCfg)
181	
182		stopUnit(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
183		delLink(t, r, edge, tunnelIface)
184		t.Cleanup(func() {
185			stopUnit(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
186			delLink(t, r, edge, tunnelIface)
187		})
188	
189		startAt := edgeClockNow(t, r, edge)
190	
191		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), smokeSSHTimeout)
192		start := fmt.Sprintf("sudo systemd-run --unit=%s --service-type=simple %s --config %s",
193			dnsEdgeUnit, smokeBin, edgeCfgPath)
194		_, err := r.Run(ctx, edge, start)
195		cancel()
196		if err != nil {
197			t.Fatalf("edge: start wanbond daemon (dial %q) failed: %v", dialHost, err)
198		}
199		t.Logf("edge: wanbond daemon started dialing %q (unit=%s)", dialHost, dnsEdgeUnit)
200	
201		if !waitRemoteLink(t, r, edge, tunnelIface, linkAppearTimeout) {
202			dumpUnitLog(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
203			t.Fatalf("edge: %s never appeared", tunnelIface)
204		}
205		addressLink(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeInner)
206	
207		// --- Resolution + handshake (REPORT-ONLY: never fails the suite) -------------------
208		handshakeOK := pingUntil(t, r, edge, dnsConcInner, dnsHandshakeTimeout)
209		var handshakeElapsed time.Duration
210		if handshakeOK {
211			handshakeElapsed = edgeClockNow(t, r, edge).Sub(startAt)
212		}
213	
214		journal := readDaemonJournal(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
215		resolvedAddr, resolveElapsed, resolveOK := firstResolutionAfter(journal, startAt)
216	
217		if resolveOK {
218			t.Logf("RESOLUTION: %q -> %s (%s after daemon start)", dialHost, resolvedAddr, resolveElapsed)
219		} else {
220			t.Logf("RESOLUTION: %q did not resolve to a usable endpoint within the window", dialHost)
221		}
222		if handshakeOK {
223			t.Logf("HANDSHAKE: OK (time-to-first-handshake ~ %s)", handshakeElapsed.Round(time.Millisecond))
224		} else {
225			t.Logf("HANDSHAKE: did not complete within %s", dnsHandshakeTimeout)
226		}
227	
228		// --- Steady traffic (REPORT-ONLY; skipped entirely when there was no handshake) ----
229		var mbps float64
230		trafficOK := false
231		if handshakeOK {
232			mbps, trafficOK = dnsTrafficSample(t, r, edge, dnsConcInner)
233		}
234		if trafficOK {
235			t.Logf("TRAFFIC: %.2f Mbit/s over the dial-by-name tunnel", mbps)
236		} else {
237			t.Logf("TRAFFIC: no sample (handshake=%t)", handshakeOK)
238		}
239	
240		// Final report block (report-only; nothing above or below gates the subtest).
241		t.Logf("=== DNS DIAL-BY-NAME RESULT ===\n"+
242			"  dial host:       %s\n"+
243			"  resolved:        %t (%s)\n"+
244			"  handshake:       %t (%s)\n"+
245			"  traffic sample:  %t (%.2f Mbit/s)",
246			dialHost, resolveOK, resolvedAddr, handshakeOK, handshakeElapsed.Round(time.Millisecond), trafficOK, mbps)
247	}
248	
249	// dumpUnitLog logs the tail of unit's journal on host for diagnosing a failed
250	// bring-up. A parameterized sibling of dumpDaemonLog (which hardcodes smokeUnit),
251	// needed here because this tier's daemons run under their own unit names.
252	func dumpUnitLog(t *testing.T, r *Runner, host Host, unit string) {
253		t.Helper()
254		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), smokeSSHTimeout)
255		defer cancel()
256		res, err := r.Run(ctx, host, "sudo journalctl -u "+unit+" --no-pager -n 50 2>&1; true")
257		if err != nil {
258			t.Logf("%s: could not read %s journal: %v", host.Role, unit, err)
259			return
260		}
261		t.Logf("%s: %s journal (last 50 lines):\n%s", host.Role, unit, res.Stdout)
262	}
263	
264	// dnsResolutionRecord is the subset of the "hub failover: first endpoint resolution"
265	// slog line (internal/device/failover.go) this tier reads: it is logged exactly once,
266	// the moment the re-resolution controller installs the FIRST successfully resolved
267	// address on the engine peer.
268	type dnsResolutionRecord struct {
269		Time       time.Time `json:"time"`
270		Msg        string    `json:"msg"`
271		ToEndpoint string    `json:"to_endpoint"`
272	}
273	
274	// firstResolutionAfter scans journal for the "first endpoint resolution" transition
275	// logged strictly after `after` and returns the address it installed and the elapsed
276	// time from `after` to that transition. ok is false when no such transition appears
277	// — i.e. the hostname never resolved to a usable address in the window, which is
278	// exactly what the bogus_name subtest expects to observe and report.
279	func firstResolutionAfter(journal string, after time.Time) (endpoint string, elapsed time.Duration, ok bool) {
280		for _, line := range strings.Split(journal, "\n") {
281			if !strings.Contains(line, "first endpoint resolution") {
282				continue
283			}
284			var rec dnsResolutionRecord
285			if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(line), &rec); err != nil {
286				continue
287			}
288			if rec.ToEndpoint == "" || !rec.Time.After(after) {
289				continue
290			}
291			return rec.ToEndpoint, rec.Time.Sub(after), true
292		}
293		return "", 0, false
294	}
295	
296	// dnsTrafficSample runs a short best-effort iperf3 TCP sample from edge to the
297	// concentrator's inner IP and reports the achieved throughput. Unlike iperfTCP
298	// (smoke tier), every failure is LOGGED and returns ok=false rather than
299	// t.Fatalf — the whole point of this tier is that a data-plane sample taken after a
300	// bogus-name dial REPORTS, it never fails the suite.
301	func dnsTrafficSample(t *testing.T, r *Runner, edge Host, serverIP string) (mbps float64, ok bool) {
302		t.Helper()
303		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), dnsTrafficTimeout)
304		defer cancel()
305		res, err := r.Run(ctx, edge, fmt.Sprintf("iperf3 -c %s -t 5 -J", serverIP))
306		if err != nil {
307			t.Logf("edge: dial-by-name traffic sample failed: %v", err)
308			return 0, false
309		}
310		var rep iperfReport
311		if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(res.Stdout), &rep); err != nil {
312			t.Logf("edge: parse dial-by-name iperf3 JSON failed: %v\n%s", err, res.Stdout)
313			return 0, false
314		}
315		return rep.End.SumSent.BitsPerSecond / 1e6, true
316	}
317

1	//go:build realhosts
2	
3	package realhosts
4	
5	import (
6		"bytes"
7		"context"
8		"fmt"
9		"os"
10		"os/exec"
11		"strconv"
12		"strings"
13		"time"
14	)
15	
16	// Environment variables that select the real hosts and credentials. Each has a
17	// default (below) so the tier runs against the standing worker machines with no
18	// configuration.
19	const (
20		envEdgeHost  = "WANBOND_EDGE_HOST"
21		envConcHost  = "WANBOND_CONC_HOST"
22		envConcPubIP = "WANBOND_CONC_PUBLIP"
23		envSSHKey    = "WANBOND_SSH_KEY"
24	)
25	
26	// Defaults for the standing two-machine testbed:
27	//   - the edge sits behind a symmetric NAT and is amd64;
28	//   - the concentrator is aarch64 and has a public, inbound-reachable IP.
29	const (
30		defaultEdgeHost  = "llm-ubuntu-0.pgtr.7mind.io"
31		defaultConcHost  = "o3.7mind.io"
32		defaultConcPubIP = "89.168.124.91"
33		defaultSSHKey    = "/run/agenix/llm-ssh-key"
34	
35		// defaultUser is the login shared by both worker machines.
36		defaultUser = "ubuntu"
37	
38		// defaultConnectTimeout bounds the TCP+auth handshake of a single ssh
39		// invocation. The runner passes it as `-o ConnectTimeout=<n>` (seconds).
40		defaultConnectTimeout = 10 * time.Second
41	)
42	
43	// Host is one real machine reachable over SSH.
44	type Host struct {
45		// Role is a human label for the machine's function ("edge"/"concentrator").
46		Role string
47		// Addr is the SSH target hostname (or IP) passed to ssh.
48		Addr string
49		// User is the SSH login user.
50		User string
51	}
52	
53	// target renders the user@host argument ssh expects.
54	func (h Host) target() string { return h.User + "@" + h.Addr }
55	
56	// Config is the resolved real-host topology plus SSH credentials, populated from
57	// the environment (with defaults) by LoadConfig.
58	type Config struct {
59		Edge Host
60		Conc Host
61		// ConcPubIP is the concentrator's public, inbound-reachable IP. The edge
62		// (behind symmetric NAT) dials this; recorded here for later tasks.
63		ConcPubIP string
64		// SSHKey is the private-key path handed to `ssh -i`.
65		SSHKey string
66		// ConnectTimeout bounds each ssh connect.
67		ConnectTimeout time.Duration
68	}
69	
70	// envOr returns the value of env var key, or def when it is unset or empty.
71	func envOr(key, def string) string {
72		if v := os.Getenv(key); v != "" {
73			return v
74		}
75		return def
76	}
77	
78	// LoadConfig resolves the real-host topology from the environment, applying the
79	// standing-testbed defaults for any unset variable. Both hosts use the default
80	// SSH login user.
81	func LoadConfig() Config {
82		return Config{
83			Edge:           Host{Role: "edge", Addr: envOr(envEdgeHost, defaultEdgeHost), User: defaultUser},
84			Conc:           Host{Role: "concentrator", Addr: envOr(envConcHost, defaultConcHost), User: defaultUser},
85			ConcPubIP:      envOr(envConcPubIP, defaultConcPubIP),
86			SSHKey:         envOr(envSSHKey, defaultSSHKey),
87			ConnectTimeout: defaultConnectTimeout,
88		}
89	}
90	
91	// Result captures the outcome of one remote command.
92	type Result struct {
93		Host   Host
94		Cmd    string
95		Stdout string
96		Stderr string
97	}
98	
99	// Runner executes commands on real hosts over SSH. It is safe for sequential
100	// use; the SSH invocation itself carries no shared state.
101	type Runner struct {
102		key            string
103		connectTimeout time.Duration
104	}
105	
106	// NewRunner builds a Runner from a Config's key and connect timeout.
107	func NewRunner(cfg Config) *Runner {
108		return &Runner{key: cfg.SSHKey, connectTimeout: cfg.ConnectTimeout}
109	}
110	
111	// sshArgs assembles the argv for a single ssh invocation against host running
112	// remoteCmd. The system ssh_config is known-broken on these workers, so `-F
113	// none` forces ssh to ignore it; the remaining options pin the identity file,
114	// auto-accept the host key on first contact, and bound the connect.
115	func (r *Runner) sshArgs(host Host, remoteCmd string) []string {
116		secs := int(r.connectTimeout / time.Second)
117		if secs < 1 {
118			secs = 1
119		}
120		return []string{
121			"-F", "none",
122			"-i", r.key,
123			"-o", "StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new",
124			"-o", "ConnectTimeout=" + strconv.Itoa(secs),
125			host.target(),
126			remoteCmd,
127		}
128	}
129	
130	// Run executes remoteCmd on host over SSH, capturing stdout and stderr
131	// separately. A non-nil error wraps the exit status and includes the captured
132	// stderr for diagnosis; ctx bounds the whole invocation.
133	func (r *Runner) Run(ctx context.Context, host Host, remoteCmd string) (Result, error) {
134		cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "ssh", r.sshArgs(host, remoteCmd)...)
135		var stdout, stderr bytes.Buffer
136		cmd.Stdout, cmd.Stderr = &stdout, &stderr
137	
138		res := Result{Host: host, Cmd: remoteCmd}
139		err := cmd.Run()
140		res.Stdout, res.Stderr = strings.TrimRight(stdout.String(), "\n"), strings.TrimRight(stderr.String(), "\n")
141		if err != nil {
142			return res, fmt.Errorf("ssh %s: %q: %w\nstderr: %s", host.target(), remoteCmd, err, res.Stderr)
143		}
144		return res, nil
145	}
146

content.content:
280		if specIdx < 0 || specIdx >= len(h.specs) {
281			return
282		}
283		h.specs[specIdx].addrs = addrs
284	
285		switch {
286		case h.activeSpec == -1:
287			// No active entry yet (every spec was empty at boot). If this resolution populated
288			// the flattened list, adopt its head, point the bond at it, and arm the settle dwell.
289			if adoptSpec, adoptAddr := h.entryAtLocked(0); adoptSpec != -1 {
290				h.activeSpec = adoptSpec
291				h.activeAddr = adoptAddr
292				h.lastSwitch = h.clock.Now()
293				// FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70): the peer booted endpoint-less, so the engine peer
294				// has NO endpoint yet. INSTALL the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via the UAPI
295				// path (install) FIRST — a SetPeerRemote repoint would move the bind's per-path remotes
296				// but leave the engine peer endpoint-less, so the following rehandshake would have no
297				// endpoint to transmit to — THEN rehandshake, which now has an addressable endpoint.
298				h.install(adoptAddr)
299				if h.rehandshake != nil {
300					h.rehandshake()
301				}
302				h.log.Warn("hub failover: first endpoint resolution; installed active concentrator endpoint on the engine peer and re-handshaked",
303					"spec_index", adoptSpec, "to_endpoint", adoptAddr.String())
304			}
305		case specIdx == h.activeSpec:
306			if !containsAddrPort(addrs, h.activeAddr) && len(addrs) > 0 {
307				// The active endpoint's own spec re-resolved off the current AddrPort: its IP
308				// changed. Repoint the bond once, re-handshake against the new first entry, and
309				// re-arm the settle dwell against the freshly-repointed endpoint.
310				prev := h.activeAddr
311				h.activeAddr = addrs[0]
312				h.lastSwitch = h.clock.Now()
313				h.remote.SetPeerRemote(h.activeAddr)
314				if h.rehandshake != nil {
315					h.rehandshake()
316				}
317				h.log.Warn("hub failover: active concentrator endpoint re-resolved; repointed remote and re-handshaked",
318					"spec_index", specIdx, "from_endpoint", prev.String(), "to_endpoint", h.activeAddr.String())
319			}
320			// Survived (or empty expansion): no repoint. idx re-maps below.
321		}
322	
323		h.idx = h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
324	}

content.text:
All checks pass. Evidence gathered:

- **Report-only discipline (a):** The only `t.Fatalf` calls (L132, L138, L197, L203) are on infra bring-up (concentrator/edge daemon start + TUN appearance). The resolution/handshake/traffic outcome path (L207-247) uses `t.Logf` exclusively. `pingUntil` returns a bool (non-fatal, smoke_test.go:372), `readDaemonJournal` is best-effort, `firstResolutionAfter` is pure, `dnsTrafficSample` logs every failure and returns `ok=false`. The `bogus_name` subtest therefore cannot fail on outcome.
- **Matches sibling discipline (b):** Reuses shared helpers `readDaemonJournal`/`edgeClockNow`; the `dnsResolutionRecord` subset-struct JSON parse exactly mirrors `livenessRecord` in multipath_failover_test.go. Daemon uses `slog.NewJSONHandler` (internal/log/log.go:43) and the journal is read via `journalctl -o cat`, so line-wise `json.Unmarshal` works. Fatal-only-on-infra-bringup matches every tier.
- **Marker parse (c):** Production logs `h.log.Warn("hub failover: first endpoint resolution; ...", "spec_index", ..., "to_endpoint", adoptAddr.String())` (failover.go:302-303) on the first-resolve install path (activeSpec == -1) — exactly the path an endpoint-less edge booting a `dns=true` hostname takes. `Contains("first endpoint resolution")`, `to_endpoint`, and slog's default RFC3339Nano `time` field all match; Warn ≥ configured `info` level so it is emitted.
- **Bogus name (d):** `validateHostname` (config.go:655) accepts all four labels of `...wanbond-dns-test.example`; endpoint validation (config.go:637) passes, so the daemon starts. `.example` is RFC 2606-reserved → deterministic NXDOMAIN → marker never logged → `resolveOK=false`, reported, subtest passes.
- **Gate (e):** `go vet -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/...` → exit 0; full non-realhosts `go build ./... && go vet ./...` → exit 0.

Change is a single surgical new file (316 lines), no production or sibling-test edits.

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T78 round 1 (diff 356d850..12a238a), one new file test/realhosts/dns_dial_test.go
- **Achieved:** verdict approve; 0 criticisms / 0 questions
- **Discovered:** report-only outcome path is Logf-only with Fatal confined to infra bring-up; JSON journal parse matches production `to_endpoint`/`time` fields and sibling `livenessRecord` pattern; bogus `.example` name passes `validateHostname` yet is deterministically NXDOMAIN; both build tags vet green
- **Issues:** the standing testbed is not runnable here, so the live report/pass was verified by construction (compile/vet + source inspection), not execution
